herbs.name: Rosemary White
herbs.description: A rare variety with white flowers, woody aroma, perfect for stews and roasts in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Boosts memory with its antioxidants perfect for Nigeria's stews or as a tea to sharpen your mind!
herbs.alert: Rosemary's tough enough to handle Nigeria's sun and dry spells while still looking great!

herbs.name: Rosemary Green
herbs.description: Classic rosemary with a pine-like scent, a staple for Nigerian cooking and memory boosts.
herbs.benefits: Improves focus and fights inflammation, add to pepper soup or brew as tea for a brainy lift!
herbs.alert: Rosemary was worn by Greek scholars to boost memory, study smarter!

herbs.name: Stevia
herbs.description: A natural sweetener with zero calories, ideal for teas and zobo in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Sweetens without sugar spikes, perfect for diabetics or a guilt-free zobo sip!
herbs.alert: Stevia's 300 times sweeter than sugar, talk about a sweet deal!

herbs.name: Lipia Alba
herbs.description: A citrus-mint herb used in calming teas, popular in Nigerian herbal blends.
herbs.benefits: Eases stress and aids digestion, brew it after a long day in Lagos traffic!
herbs.alert: Lipia Alba's camphor-like scent is a natural relaxant, chill out!

herbs.name: Garlic Chives
herbs.description: Mild garlic-flavored chives, great for egusi soup and stir-fries in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Chop into soups or sprinkle on rice, adds a garlicky kick without the bite!
herbs.alert: Garlic chives have been flavoring dishes for 4,000 years, timeless taste!

herbs.name: Onion Chives
herbs.description: Delicate onion-flavored chives, perfect for jollof rice and garnishes.
herbs.benefits: Snip over pepper soup or mix into salads for a subtle onion zing, fresh and easy!
herbs.alert: Romans used onion chives for sunburn relief, multitasking greens!

herbs.name: Holy Basil
herbs.description: Peppery and sacred, used in Nigerian teas for its calming effects.
herbs.benefits: Reduces stress and boosts immunity, sip it as tea for a holy health kick!
herbs.alert: Holy Basil's revered in India as Tulsi, temple-worthy wellness!

herbs.name: Lemon Basil
herbs.description: Citrusy basil with a zesty kick, ideal for Nigerian fish stews.
herbs.benefits: Toss into soups or blend with peppers, citrus vibes meet spicy flair!
herbs.alert: Lemon Basil's citral content mimics lemongrass, zesty twins!

herbs.name: Purple Basil
herbs.description: Spicy purple basil, a vibrant addition to Nigerian soups.
herbs.benefits: Stir into egusi or garnish rice, its color pops and flavor rocks!
herbs.alert: Purple Basil's anthocyanins shield it from UV-nature's sunglasses!

herbs.name: Peppermint
herbs.description: Cool and refreshing, perfect for Nigerian teas and breath freshening.
herbs.benefits: Soothes digestion and clears sinuses, brew it or chew it for a minty boost!
herbs.alert: Peppermint's menthol is a natural decongestant, breathe easy!

herbs.name: Chocolate Mint
herbs.description: Chocolate-scented mint, a sweet twist for Nigerian kunu drinks.
herbs.benefits: Blend into kunu or desserts, chocolate vibes without the calories!
herbs.alert: Chocolate Mint's a hybrid, wild mint meets cocoa dreams!

herbs.name: Apple Mint
herbs.description: Soft, apple-scented mint, great for zobo and fruit salads in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Mix into zobo or garnish fruits, its fuzzy leaves bring orchard freshness!
herbs.alert: Apple Mint's fuzzy leaves smell like an apple party, fruity fun!

herbs.name: Thyme
herbs.description: Tiny leaves with big flavor, a must for Nigerian stews and roasts.
herbs.benefits: Antiseptic and flavorful, add to soups or gargle as a throat, soothing tea!
herbs.alert: Thyme's thymol is in mouthwash, germ, killing flavor king!

herbs.name: Parsley
herbs.description: Vitamin-rich garnish, perfect for egusi soup and breath freshening.
herbs.benefits: Loaded with vitamins A, C, K-chop into soups or chew for fresh breath!
herbs.alert: Romans crowned winners with parsley, leafy victory vibes!

herbs.name: Cilantro
herbs.description: Zesty cilantro, a love-it-or-hate-it star in Nigerian soups.
herbs.benefits: Blend into pepper soup or salsa, its bold kick divides the crowd!
herbs.alert: Cilantro's seeds are coriander, two flavors, one plant!

herbs.name: Oregano
herbs.description: Robust oregano, a flavor bomb for Nigerian stews and pizza vibes.
herbs.benefits: Antimicrobial and tasty, sprinkle on meats or brew for a healing tea!
herbs.alert: Oregano means 'mountain joy' in Greek, peak flavor!

herbs.name: Dill
herbs.description: Tangy dill, perfect for fish stews and pickling in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Add to seafood or suya sautece, its feathery flair lifts every bite!
herbs.alert: Dill seeds kept churchgoers awake, sleepy no more!

herbs.name: Sage
herbs.description: Earthy sage, a savory boost for Nigerian meats and stuffing.
herbs.benefits: Anti, inflammatory and wise, roast with chicken or sip as tea for wellness!
herbs.alert: Sage was traded for tea in ancient China, pricey wisdom!

herbs.name: Chives
herbs.description: Mild onion chives, a garnish hero for Nigerian soups.
herbs.benefits: Snip over jollof or stew, purple blooms add flair to flavor!
herbs.alert: Chives bloom purple, garnish with sass!

herbs.name: Tarragon
herbs.description: Licorice-like tarragon, a fancy twist for Nigerian chicken.
herbs.benefits: Elevate chicken stew or sauteces, its dragon roots bring bold taste!
herbs.alert: Tarragon's name means 'little dragon'-fiery flavor!

herbs.name: Lavender
herbs.description: Floral lavender, calming for teas and desserts in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Relaxes and soothes, add to kunu or bake into cakes for a floral chill!
herbs.alert: Lavender's name comes from 'lavare'-Roman bath vibes!

herbs.name: Cherry Tomatoes
herbs.description: Snack-sized tomatoes, bursting with flavor for Nigerian salads.
herbs.benefits: Toss into stew or eat raw, little bursts of sweet-tart joy!
herbs.alert: Cherry tomatoes are berries, botanical surprise!

herbs.name: Kale
herbs.description: Nutrient-packed kale, a superfood for Nigerian smoothies.
herbs.benefits: More vitamin C than oranges, blend into smoothies or sauteté for power!
herbs.alert: Kale's a superfood champ, leafy strength in every bite!

herbs.name: Fennel
herbs.description: Licorice-flavored fennel, great for fish and teas in Nigeria.
herbs.benefits: Aids digestion and freshens breath, roast with fish or sip as tea!
herbs.alert: Fennel was chewed in ancient India, breath mint OG!

herbs.name: Indian Curry
herbs.description: Spicy curry leaves, a staple for Nigerian pepper soups.
herbs.benefits: Fry into soups or stews, its aroma kicks up the heat!
herbs.alert: Indian Curry leaves aren't curry powder, leafy impostor!

herbs.name: Jasmine
herbs.description: Fragrant jasmine, perfect for Nigerian teas and night gardens.
herbs.benefits: Brew into tea or enjoy its scent, nighttime blooms steal the show!
herbs.alert: Jasmine blooms at night, moth party central!

herbs.name: Sweet Marjoram
herbs.description: Mild oregano cousin, soothing for Nigerian soups.
herbs.benefits: Eases headaches and adds flavor, stir into stew or sip as tea!
herbs.alert: Greeks used marjoram for headaches, herb lullaby!

herbs.name: Celery
herbs.description: Crunchy celery, a fibrous boost for Nigerian stews.
herbs.benefits: Detoxes and hydrates, chop into egusi or snack raw for crunch!
herbs.alert: Celery started in marshes, swamp to superstar!

fruits.name: Strawberry
fruits.description: Sweet red berries with vitamin C, a rare treat in Nigerian smoothies.
fruits.benefits: Boosts immunity with antioxidants, blend into smoothies or snack fresh for a sweet lift!
fruits.alert: Strawberries aren't true berries, seeds on the outside fool us all!

fruits.name: Apple Anna
fruits.description: Sweet apples thriving in Nigeria's warm north, perfect for snacking.
fruits.benefits: Eat fresh or juice, warm, climate apples with a crisp, sweet bite!
fruits.alert: Anna apples were bred in Israel, sun-loving sweeties!

fruits.name: Apple Red
fruits.description: Sweet-tart red apples, a vibrant addition to Nigerian salads.
fruits.benefits: Slice into salads or bake, red blush means extra antioxidants!
fruits.alert: Red apples get their color from anthocyanins, sun-kissed beauty!

fruits.name: Apple Green
fruits.description: Tart green apples, crisp and refreshing for Nigerian juices.
fruits.benefits: Juice or bake into pies, tartness cuts through the sweetness!
fruits.alert: Green apples have less sugar, tart and sassy!

fruits.name: Apple Wambugu
fruits.description: Tropical apples from Kenya, sweet and juicy for Nigeria's climate.
fruits.benefits: Snack fresh or blend, tropical sweetness meets Nigerian sun!
fruits.alert: Wambugu apples thrive in heat-Kenya's juicy export!

fruits.name: Pomegranate
fruits.description: Ruby-red seeds packed with antioxidants, a Nigerian juice favorite.
fruits.benefits: Fights inflammation, juice it or sprinkle seeds for a tart health boost!
fruits.alert: Pomegranates average 613 seeds, count 'em for fun!

fruits.name: Fig
fruits.description: Soft, sweet figs, a northern Nigerian snack delight.
fruits.benefits: Eat fresh or dry, nature's candy with a fiber punch!
fruits.alert: Figs are 5,000 years old, ancient sweetness!

fruits.name: Pokan Mandarin
fruits.description: Easy-peel mandarins, sweet and seedless for Nigerian snacks.
fruits.benefits: Peel and eat or juice, citrus convenience at its best!
fruits.alert: Mandarins were named after Chinese officials, fancy robes!

fruits.name: Tangelo
fruits.description: Sweet-tart hybrid of tangerine and grapefruit, a Nigerian juice star.
fruits.benefits: Juice or segment, its funky neck shape screams flavor royalty!
fruits.alert: Tangelo's neck is its signature, citrus crown!

fruits.name: Mayer Lemon
fruits.description: Sweet lemons, less tart, perfect for Nigerian cakes and drinks.
fruits.benefits: Zest into cakes or juice, sweet citrus with a mandarin twist!
fruits.alert: Mayer lemons are a mandarin hybrid-China's sweet gift!

fruits.name: Eureka Lemon
fruits.description: Tart lemons available year-round, a Nigerian kitchen staple.
fruits.benefits: Squeeze into tea or marinade, tartness that never quits!
fruits.alert: Eureka lemons started in California-1850s citrus star!

fruits.name: Variegated Red Lemon
fruits.description: Pink-fleshed lemons, a stunning garnish for Nigerian dishes.
fruits.benefits: Slice for drinks or decor, pink insides steal the show!
fruits.alert: Variegated lemons hide pink flesh, party inside!

fruits.name: Assam Indian Lemon
fruits.description: Small, spicy lemons, a zingy boost for Nigerian soups.
fruits.benefits: Add to pepper soup, tiny size, big flavor punch!
fruits.alert: Assam lemons spice up Indian curries, fiery little guys!

fruits.name: Red Grape Citrus
fruits.description: Sweet-tart citrus with lycopene, perfect for Nigerian juices.
fruits.benefits: Heart, healthy lycopene, juice it red for a vibrant boost!
fruits.alert: Red Grape Citrus shares pigments with tomatoes, color cousins!

fruits.name: Calamansi Lemon
fruits.description: Tiny sour lemons, a tangy hit for Nigerian marinades.
fruits.benefits: Marinate suya or squeeze into drinks, small but mighty!
fruits.alert: Calamansi is a kumquat hybrid, tiny tang titan!

fruits.name: Washington Navel Orange
fruits.description: Seedless sweet oranges, a Nigerian snacking favorite.
fruits.benefits: Peel and eat or juice, navel's baby orange inside is adorable!
fruits.alert: Navel oranges have a mini orange inside, cute quirk!

fruits.name: Valencia Orange
fruits.description: A juicy orange prized for its sweetness and high juice content. In Nigeria, it's widely juiced or eaten fresh, especially in urban markets.
fruits.benefits: Juice Valencia oranges for a sweet drink or use in fruit salads. In Nigeria, they're a juicy staple, adding sunshine to meals and refreshments!
fruits.alert: Valencia oranges are the world's top juice orange, named after the Spanish city of Valencia.

fruits.name: Kumquat Orange
fruits.description: A small, edible-peel orange with a sweet-tart flavor. In Nigeria, it's eaten whole as a snack or used in fruit mixes for its unique taste.
fruits.benefits: Eat kumquats whole for a burst of flavor or mix with mangoes. In Nigeria, they're a quirky snack, adding a sweet peel and tart flesh to your day!
fruits.alert: Kumquats are the only citrus fruit where the peel is sweeter than the flesh.

fruits.name: Clementine Orange
fruits.description: A small, seedless orange with a sweet, mild flavor. In Nigeria, it's a convenient snack and added to local fruit salads or juices.
fruits.benefits: Peel clementines for an easy snack or juice with pineapple. In Nigeria, their sweetness makes them a kid, friendly treat and a salad staple!
fruits.alert: Clementines were discovered by a French missionary, Father Clément, in Algeria in the early 1900s.

fruits.name: Mulberry
fruits.description: Dark, sweet berries rich in iron and antioxidants. In Nigeria, mulberries are eaten fresh or dried, and used in northern snacks or herbal teas.
fruits.benefits: Mulberries support blood health with iron and fight aging with antioxidants. In Nigeria, dry them for snacks or brew into tea for a nutritious boost!
fruits.alert: Mulberry trees were cultivated by the Chinese for 5,000 years to feed silkworms for silk production.

fruits.name: Pakistan Long Mulberry
fruits.description: An elongated mulberry variety with a sweet, juicy taste. In Nigeria, it's consumed fresh or dried, popular in northern regions as a snack.
fruits.benefits: Dry Pakistan long mulberries for a sweet snack or mix with nuts. In Nigeria's north, they're a juicy treat, perfect for kids and adults alike!
fruits.alert: Pakistan Long Mulberries can grow up to 3 inches long, much larger than typical mulberries.

fruits.name: Blackberry
fruits.description: Dark, tart berries packed with vitamin C and fiber. In Nigeria, they're eaten fresh or used in juices and local desserts when available.
fruits.benefits: Blackberries are rich in antioxidants, supporting heart health and reducing inflammation. In Nigeria, blend them into smoothies or eat fresh for a tart, nutritious snack, perfect for a healthy boost!
fruits.alert: Blackberries were used by ancient Greeks to treat gout due to their anti, inflammatory properties.

fruits.name: Soursop
fruits.description: A tropical fruit with creamy, tart flesh, rich in vitamin C and antioxidants. In Nigeria, it's juiced or eaten fresh, and used in herbal medicine for immunity.
fruits.benefits: Soursop's antioxidants may boost immunity and energy. In Nigeria, juice it with ginger for a refreshing drink or eat the flesh as a natural remedy, popular for its wellness properties!
fruits.alert: Soursop seeds contain annonacin, a compound studied for its potential anti, cancer properties.

fruits.name: Custard Apple
fruits.description: Also known as cherimoya, it has a creamy, sweet flesh. In Nigeria, it's eaten fresh and called Sweetsop locally, valued for its nutritional benefits.
fruits.benefits: Scoop custard apple flesh as a sweet snack or blend into smoothies. In Nigeria, it's a beloved treat, often enjoyed fresh or mixed with local fruits for a creamy delight!
fruits.alert: Custard apples were prized by the Incas and are nicknamed 'the fruit of the gods.'

fruits.name: Sweetsop
fruits.description: Also called sugar apple, this fruit has a sweet, segmented flesh. In Nigeria, it's a popular snack and juiced.
fruits.benefits: Eat sweetsop fresh for a sugary treat or juice it with lime. In Nigeria, it's a street snack favorite, loved for its sweet, custard-like texture!
fruits.alert: Sweetsop's scientific name, Annona squamosa, refers to its scaly skin resembling a pine cone.

fruits.name: Cherry
fruits.description: Small, sweet-tart fruits packed with antioxidants and vitamin C. In Nigeria, cherries are a rare treat, eaten fresh or used in imported-style desserts.
fruits.benefits: Eat cherries fresh as a premium snack or top cakes with them. In Nigeria, their rarity makes them a special addition to fruit salads or desserts!
fruits.alert: Cherries were brought to Rome from Turkey in 72 BCE by a general named Lucullus.

fruits.name: Plum
fruits.description: Juicy, sweet-tart plums rich in vitamins A and C. In Nigeria, they're eaten fresh or dried into prunes, occasionally used in northern fruit mixes.
fruits.benefits: Plums aid digestion and boost immunity with their vitamin content. In Nigeria, enjoy them fresh or dried as prunes for a fiber, rich snack!
fruits.alert: Plums were domesticated in China over 2,000 years ago and are related to peaches and almonds.

fruits.name: Yellow Plum
fruits.description: A sweeter, golden variety of plum with juicy, tender flesh. In Nigeria, it's a premium snack and could be used in local jams or fruit salads.
fruits.benefits: Turn yellow plums into jam or slice into salads. In Nigeria, their sweetness makes them a delightful addition to fruit mixes or a standalone treat!
fruits.alert: Yellow plums are often sweeter because they ripen longer on the tree than darker varieties.

fruits.name: Dwarf Hass Avocado
fruits.description: A compact version of the Hass avocado, with creamy, nutty flesh. In Nigeria, it's mashed into spreads or eaten with bread, valued for its healthy fats.
fruits.benefits: Dwarf Hass avocados are packed with healthy fats that support heart health. In Nigeria, mash them with bread or blend into smoothies for a creamy, nutritious boost!
fruits.alert: Hass avocados are named after Rudolph Hass, a mailman who patented the variety in 1935.

fruits.name: Jumbo White Guava
fruits.description: A large, white-fleshed guava with a sweet, mild flavor. In Nigeria, it's eaten fresh or juiced for its vitamin C.
fruits.benefits: Jumbo white guavas are loaded with vitamin C, great for immunity. In Nigeria, juice them or eat fresh for a sweet, healthy snack that fights colds!
fruits.alert: Guavas have more vitamin C per gram than oranges and were used by sailors to prevent scurvy.

fruits.name: Seeded Grape
fruits.description: Traditional grapes with seeds, offering a sweet, juicy taste. In Nigeria, they're eaten fresh or used in northern snacks and fruit blends.
fruits.benefits: Eat seeded grapes fresh or mix with nuts for a snack. In Nigeria's north, they're a juicy addition to fruit salads, bringing a classic grape flavor!
fruits.alert: Grapes were one of the first fruits to be fermented into wine, over 8,000 years ago.

fruits.name: Seedless Grape
fruits.description: Sweet, seedless grapes perfect for easy snacking. In Nigeria, they're a premium snack, often imported, and used in fruit salads or juices.
fruits.benefits: Enjoy seedless grapes as a convenient snack or toss into salads. In Nigeria, their ease and sweetness make them a luxury treat for all ages!
fruits.alert: Seedless grapes are created through selective breeding, not genetic modification.

fruits.name: Dwarf Pawpaw
fruits.description: A smaller papaya variety with sweet, orange flesh. In Nigeria, it's eaten fresh or blended into smoothies.
fruits.benefits: Dwarf pawpaw's papain aids digestion, and its vitamins boost immunity. In Nigeria, enjoy it fresh or in smoothies for a tropical, healthy treat!
fruits.alert: Papayas contain papain, an enzyme that helps digest proteins and is used in meat tenderizers.

fruits.name: Banana
fruits.description: Bananas are potassium-packed powerhouses, perfect for a quick energy boost. They're technically berries-how a-peeling is that? In Nigeria, they're a staple snack.
fruits.benefits: Eat bananas fresh or fry them as plantain chips. In Nigeria, they're a versatile staple, enjoyed alone or paired with groundnut for a classic combo!
fruits.alert: Bananas are the world's most consumed fruit and grow upside, down, pointing upward.

fruits.name: Watermelon
fruits.description: Watermelons are hydration heroes, 92% water, with a sweet crunch that's summer in every bite. They're the melon-choly cure you need! In Nigeria, they're a refreshing snack.
fruits.benefits: Watermelon hydrates and provides lycopene for heart health. In Nigeria, enjoy it fresh or juice it for a cool, sweet treat, perfect for hot days!
fruits.alert: Watermelons were first grown in Egypt 5,000 years ago and depicted in tomb paintings.
